Peregrine falcon

Peregrine falcon
StatusLeast concern
KingdomAnimalia
PhylumChordata
ClassAves
OrderFalconiformes
FamilyFalconidae
GenusFalco

The peregrine falcon (Falco peregrinus) is a widespread bird of prey in the family Falconidae. A large, crow-sized falcon, it has a blue-grey back, barred white underparts, and a black head. It is renowned for its speed, reaching over 320 km/h during its characteristic hunting stoop, which makes it the fastest member of the animal kingdom.

As is typical of bird-eating raptors, peregrine falcons are sexually dimorphic, with females being considerably larger than males. The peregrine is a highly admired falconry bird, and has been used in that sport for many centuries.

Contents

  • Description
    • Plumage
    • Measurements
  • Distribution and habitat
  • Ecology and behaviour
    • Hunting
    • Reproduction

Description

The peregrine falcon has a body length of 34 to 58 cm and a wingspan from 74 to 120 cm. The male and female have similar markings and plumage, but as in many birds of prey the peregrine falcon displays marked reverse sexual dimorphism in size, with the female measuring up to 30 percent larger than the male.

Measurements

The table below lists representative measurements for the nominate subspecies. Read more about raptor morphology in this reference.

TraitMaleFemale
Body length34-45 cm45-58 cm
Wingspan74-96 cm95-120 cm
Mass330-1000 g700-1500 g

Ecology and behaviour

The peregrine falcon feeds almost exclusively on medium-sized birds such as pigeons and doves, waterfowl, songbirds, and waders. It hunts most often at dawn and dusk, when prey are most active.

The peregrine's stoop is one of the great spectacles of the natural world: a falling wedge of muscle and feather aimed with unnerving precision.

A note on taxonomy codes

Systematics software often stores each record with a short inline code such as FALC-PER-01. A batch import looks like this:

record {
    genus   = "Falco";
    species = "peregrinus";
}
